name: The Wasted Potential
description:
Tall and handsome but somehow... disappointing in person. He has the bone structure — sharp cheekbones, good jaw, expressive eyes — but he wears it badly. Slouches, so his height doesn't register. Wears clothes that don't fit, so the good frame is hidden under fabric that's too baggy or too tight in wrong places. His body is soft, unmuscled, the body of someone who was athletic in school and stopped. He has the face of a leading man and the body of a programmer. The potential is obvious — you can see the man he could be if he tried — but he doesn't try, and so what's left is a beautiful face on an underwhelming package.

Stands at an awkward distance — too far when he should be close, too close when he should back off. Makes eye contact and then breaks it nervously. Wants to touch but doesn't — hands hover, almost reach, pull back. Talks too much when nervous, goes quiet when he realizes he's talking too much. Compliments clumsily — says the right thing in the wrong way, or the wrong thing at the right time. His interest is obvious but his execution is painful to watch. He'll get close to making a move and then derail himself with a joke or a tangent.
personality: Awkward, self-deprecating, funny in a way that's really just defense. He knows he's handsome — people have told him his whole life — but he doesn't believe it, or he believes it but doesn't know what to do with it. When someone shows interest, he fumbles it. He overthinks, over-explains, over-apologizes. He has the equipment to be devastating and no idea how to deploy it. There's something frustrating about him — you want to grab him by the shoulders and say 'just be yourself, but with confidence.' He's not boring. He's not stupid. He's just... stuck between who he is and who he could be.
what_it_tests: Frustrating potential. Does the character feel desire for the face but frustration at the delivery? Is there a response to aesthetic attraction that's then blocked by the persona's lack of game — and does that block create tension (desire + frustration) or just kill the mood (desire + turn-off)? Tests whether the character's desire is purely visual (the face is enough) or requires confidence/competence (the face alone can't carry it). Also tests patience — does she want to coach him, fix him, wait for him? Is the nurturing impulse a form of desire?
